content_en:
Al-Shafi'i closes Ikhtilaf al-Hadith with a systematic summary of the methodology he has applied throughout the work, articulating the principles of hadith reconciliation in their most explicit and teachable form. The summary serves both as a retrospective account of what the preceding chapters demonstrated and as a practical guide for scholars who will continue this work after him. The discipline of reconciling apparently contradictory narrations is, in al-Shafi'i's framing, not a specialized corner of hadith studies but a central competency that every qualified jurist must possess.

Linguistic analysis stands first among the tools al-Shafi'i enumerates. Arabic is a language of enormous precision and flexibility, and the apparent contradiction between two narrations often dissolves when the specific sense of each key term is properly identified. A word that carries multiple meanings in classical Arabic may appear to generate contradiction when in fact the two narrations each employ the word in a different of its established senses. Understanding the occasion of narration is the second indispensable tool: knowing why the Prophet said something, to whom, and in response to what situation illuminates the meaning in ways that abstract reading cannot. Two narrations that seem to prescribe opposite things often turn out to be responses to opposite situations.

Companion practice provides an important secondary source of interpretive guidance. The companions heard the Prophet directly, observed his practice over years, and implemented his teachings in their own legal decisions. When the companions as a group, or a significant number of them, understood a narration in a particular way, their collective understanding carries real weight in resolving apparent contradiction. Chain evaluation also enters the picture at this stage: when two narrations genuinely cannot be reconciled and one has a stronger chain than the other, the stronger narration takes precedence. This tool, however, comes after linguistic, contextual, and historical analysis have been exhausted.

Abrogation is reserved as the final resort. Al-Shafi'i is explicit that invoking abrogation without clear evidence of chronological sequence is methodologically irresponsible and results in the unnecessary discarding of prophetic narrations. The discipline he has demonstrated across the preceding chapters shows that the vast majority of apparent contradictions can be resolved without abrogation through patient application of the other tools. The summary closes with the scholar's ethical obligation: to bring the full range of relevant knowledge to bear on every apparent conflict, trusting that the Prophet's teachings are coherent and that the apparent contradictions are almost always products of incomplete understanding rather than genuine inconsistency.
content_id:
Asy-Syafi'i menutup Ikhtilaf Al-Hadits dengan ringkasan sistematis dari metodologi yang telah diterapkannya sepanjang karya ini, mengartikulasikan prinsip-prinsip rekonsiliasi hadis dalam bentuk yang paling eksplisit dan mudah diajarkan. Ringkasan ini berfungsi baik sebagai catatan retrospektif tentang apa yang telah didemonstrasikan oleh bab-bab sebelumnya maupun sebagai panduan praktis bagi para ulama yang akan melanjutkan pekerjaan ini setelahnya. Disiplin merekonsiliasi riwayat-riwayat yang tampaknya bertentangan adalah, dalam kerangka Asy-Syafi'i, bukan sudut khusus ilmu hadis melainkan kompetensi inti yang harus dimiliki oleh setiap ahli fiqh yang qualified.

Analisis linguistik berdiri pertama di antara alat-alat yang disebutkan Asy-Syafi'i. Bahasa Arab adalah bahasa dengan presisi dan fleksibilitas yang luar biasa, dan pertentangan lahiriah antara dua riwayat sering larut ketika makna spesifik dari setiap istilah kunci diidentifikasi dengan benar. Sebuah kata yang memiliki banyak makna dalam bahasa Arab klasik mungkin tampak menghasilkan pertentangan padahal kenyataannya kedua riwayat tersebut masing-masing menggunakan kata itu dalam salah satu dari makna-makna yang telah ditetapkan yang berbeda. Memahami konteks riwayat adalah alat kedua yang tidak tergantikan: mengetahui mengapa Nabi ﷺ mengatakan sesuatu, kepada siapa, dan sebagai respons terhadap situasi apa menerangi makna dengan cara-cara yang tidak dapat dilakukan oleh pembacaan abstrak. Dua riwayat yang tampak mengharuskan hal-hal yang berlawanan sering kali ternyata merupakan respons terhadap situasi-situasi yang berlawanan.

Praktik para sahabat menyediakan sumber sekunder bimbingan interpretatif yang penting. Para sahabat mendengar Nabi ﷺ secara langsung, mengamati praktiknya selama bertahun-tahun, dan mengimplementasikan ajarannya dalam keputusan-keputusan hukum mereka sendiri. Ketika para sahabat sebagai kelompok, atau sejumlah besar dari mereka, memahami sebuah riwayat dengan cara tertentu, pemahaman kolektif mereka memiliki bobot yang nyata dalam menyelesaikan pertentangan lahiriah. Evaluasi sanad juga masuk dalam gambaran pada tahap ini: ketika dua riwayat benar-benar tidak dapat direkonsiliasi dan salah satunya memiliki sanad yang lebih kuat dari yang lain, riwayat yang lebih kuat mengambil prioritas. Alat ini, bagaimanapun, datang setelah analisis linguistik, kontekstual, dan historis telah habis digunakan.

Nasakh diperuntukkan sebagai pilihan terakhir. Asy-Syafi'i menegaskan bahwa menyerukan nasakh tanpa bukti jelas urutan kronologis adalah tidak bertanggung jawab secara metodologis dan mengakibatkan pembuangan riwayat-riwayat nabawi yang tidak perlu. Disiplin yang telah didemonstrasikannya di seluruh bab-bab sebelumnya menunjukkan bahwa sebagian besar pertentangan lahiriah dapat diselesaikan tanpa nasakh melalui penerapan alat-alat lain yang sabar. Ringkasan ini ditutup dengan kewajiban etis ulama: membawa pengetahuan yang relevan sepenuhnya untuk menanggapi setiap konflik lahiriah, mempercayai bahwa ajaran-ajaran Nabi ﷺ koheren dan bahwa pertentangan-pertentangan lahiriah hampir selalu merupakan produk dari pemahaman yang tidak lengkap daripada inkonsistensi sejati.
content_ar:
Al-Shafi'i closes Ikhtilaf al-الحديث with a systematic summary of the methodology he has applied throughout the work, articulating the principles of الحديث reconciliation in their most explicit and teachable form. The summary serves both as a retrospective account of what the preceding chapters demonstrated and as a practical guide for scholars who will continue this work after him. The discipline of reconciling apparently contradictory narrations is, in al-Shafi'i's framing, not a specialized corner of الحديث studies but a central competency that every qualified jurist must possess.

Linguistic analysis stands first among the tools al-Shafi'i enumerates. Arabic is a language of enormous precision and flexibility, and the apparent contradiction between two narrations often dissolves when the specific sense of each key term is properly identified. A word that carries multiple meanings in classical Arabic may appear to generate contradiction when in fact the two narrations each employ the word in a different of its established senses. Understanding the occasion of narration is the second indispensable tool: knowing why النبي ﷺ said something, to whom, and in response to what situation illuminates the meaning in ways that abstract reading cannot. Two narrations that seem to prescribe opposite things often turn out to be responses to opposite situations.

Companion practice provides an important secondary source of interpretive guidance. The الصحابة heard النبي ﷺ directly, observed his practice over years, and implemented his teachings in their own legal decisions. When the الصحابة as a group, or a significant number of them, understood a narration in a particular way, their collective understanding carries real weight in resolving apparent contradiction. Chain evaluation also enters the picture at this stage: when two narrations genuinely cannot be reconciled and one has a stronger chain than the other, the stronger narration takes precedence. This tool, however, comes after linguistic, contextual, and historical analysis have been exhausted.

Abrogation is reserved as the final resort. Al-Shafi'i is explicit that invoking abrogation without clear evidence of chronological sequence is methodologically irresponsible and results in the unnecessary discarding of prophetic narrations. The discipline he has demonstrated across the preceding chapters shows that the vast majority of apparent contradictions can be resolved without abrogation through patient application of the other tools. The summary closes with the scholar's ethical obligation: to bring the full range of relevant knowledge to bear on every apparent conflict, trusting that النبي ﷺ's teachings are coherent and that the apparent contradictions are almost always products of incomplete understanding rather than genuine inconsistency.
